title: Life expectancy, including UN projections
subtitle: The [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.
caption:
Life expectancy, including UN projections
The period life expectancy at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.

The line chart tracks period life expectancy at birth from 1950 through 2100, measuring the average number of years a newborn is expected to live based on current mortality rates. The vertical axis ranges from 0 to 100 years, while the horizontal axis spans the timeline in 20-year increments. Solid lines represent observed historical data up to approximately 2020, while dotted lines indicate medium-variant projections extending to the end of the century. Japan consistently occupies the highest position on the chart, starting near 60 years in 1950 and rising to roughly 85 years by 2020, with projections placing it above 90 years by 2100. Sweden follows a similar high trajectory, beginning around 70 years and projected to reach nearly 90 years. In contrast, India starts at the lowest point among the named countries, just above 40 years in 1950, climbing to approximately 70 years by 2020, with projections showing continued growth toward 85 years. Brazil and the World average follow intermediate paths; the global average rises from roughly 48 years to 73 years by 2020, with projections estimating an increase to over 80 years by 2100. A distinct dip is visible in the data for India and the World average around 2020, interrupting the otherwise steady upward trend before the lines resume their ascent in the projection phase. The visual pattern shows a general convergence over time, as countries with lower initial life expectancies experience steeper rates of increase compared to those that started with higher values, narrowing the gap between the highest and lowest lines by the year 2100.
